acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than 4 instances of 'the way [X]' as a comparison/simile structure. Each remaining instance uses a different vehicle — no two share the same metaphor family (e.g., not two organic/natural analogies back-to-back). The proxy-office section (lines 49-105) and the evening domestic section (lines 115-225) each contain no more than 2.
evidence: The comparative construction 'the way [X]' appears 12+ times across the chapter. Representative instances: 'the way you might leave a cabinet open' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:3), 'the way signing things and nodding constituted his' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:19), 'the way the suit was, the way the commute was' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:53, two in one sentence), 'the way a current structures a river' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:105), 'the way you absorb a heartbeat' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:117), 'the way a river organizes its banks' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:123), 'the way he knew the radiator's knock' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:135), 'the way you say something when you're not sure' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:213). Additional instances at lines 31, 49, 115.
problem: The 'the way [X]' comparison has become the chapter's default simile frame, appearing roughly once every 18 lines. The construction works well individually — it conveys Dan's habit of understanding his present through analogy — but at 12+ instances it becomes a predictable rhythmic signature. The reader begins anticipating the construction, which flattens the prose from observational to mannered. By the chapter's second half, each new 'the way' arrival feels less like narrative voice and more like autopilot.
rewrite_direction: Reduce 'the way [X]' comparisons to no more than 4 total, distributed across the chapter's two scenes. In the proxy-office section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:49-105), which currently contains 5+ instances, retain at most 2. In the evening domestic section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:115-225), retain at most 2. For removed instances, use varied alternatives: direct rendering of the comparison without the 'the way' frame, sensory detail that makes the analogy implicit, or entirely different syntactic structures (apposition, embedded metaphor, physical action). The line 53 double use ('the way the suit was, the way the commute was') is the strongest candidate for retention because the repetition there is rhetorical rather than habitual.

acceptance_test: The revised chapter uses 'the specific [noun]' and 'the particular [noun]' no more than 5 times combined. The line 107 sentence retains at most one instance of 'specific.' Each remaining instance is justified by a following clause that specifies what makes the thing specific or particular — the modifier does work beyond merely promising precision.
evidence: 'The specific [noun]' appears 6 times: 'the specific hollow feeling' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:85), 'the specific pleasure...the specific weight...the specific satisfaction' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:107, three in one sentence), 'the specific slick glass feel' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:139), 'the specific emptiness' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:225). 'The particular [noun]' appears 6 times: 'the particular quality of a room' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:31), 'the particular late-February cold' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:33), 'a particular way of watching' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:71), 'a particular dialect of English' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:87), 'the particular dry precision' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:129), 'the particular quality of a child's world' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:185). Combined: 12 instances.
problem: The precision-modifier construction 'the specific/particular [noun]' functions as a verbal tic rather than a narrative tool. The words promise exactitude but the actual precision comes from the nouns and their contexts, not from the modifier — 'the hollow feeling' is no less precise than 'the specific hollow feeling.' At 12 combined instances, the construction signals that the narrator reaches for the same grammatical handle whenever rendering interiority, which paradoxically makes the prose feel less precise by making every observation sound the same. Line 107, which uses 'specific' three times in one sentence ('the specific pleasure,' 'the specific weight,' 'the specific satisfaction'), concentrates the tic into a single passage that reads as stylistic compulsion.
rewrite_direction: Reduce combined 'the specific [noun]' and 'the particular [noun]' to no more than 5 total. The line 107 triple (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:107) must retain at most one 'specific' — let the other two observations earn their precision through sensory detail rather than declaration. For removed instances, trust the nouns: 'the hollow feeling of having performed a function he couldn't name' is stronger than 'the specific hollow feeling.' Where genuine precision is needed, render it through concrete detail rather than announcing it with a modifier. Consider retaining 'specific/particular' only where the modifier creates a meaningful contrast — e.g., 'the particular quality of a room with one less body' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:31) where 'particular' earns its keep because the sentence goes on to specify what's particular.

acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains at most one 'not X, not Y, just/but Z' negation-then-definition structure. The closing passage (line 225) and the post-meeting passage (line 85) no longer share the same syntactic frame — they approach their respective emotional states through recognizably different sentence architectures.
evidence: Three instances of 'not X, not Y, just/but Z' negation-then-definition: (1) 'not dramatic, just persistent, the kind that found the gaps in your coat' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:33), (2) 'not sadness, not frustration, not even boredom exactly, but the specific hollow feeling of having performed a function he couldn't name' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:85), (3) 'not painful exactly, not unbearable, just empty, the specific emptiness of a container that had once held something and now held only the shape of what used to be there' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:225).
problem: The negation-then-definition formula is used three times, and instances 2 and 3 are structurally near-identical: both open with 'not [emotion], not [emotion],' pivot on 'but/just,' and resolve into a 'the specific [noun]' construction. The first instance (line 33) describes weather; the second (line 85) describes Dan's post-meeting emptiness; the third (line 225) describes his end-of-day emotional state. Since instances 2 and 3 both describe varieties of the same feeling (ambient emotional emptiness) in the same syntactic frame, the chapter's two major reflective moments — the midday hollow and the final-page summary — mirror each other so closely they flatten into one observation repeated.
rewrite_direction: Retain at most one instance of the 'not X, not Y, just Z' pattern. The strongest candidate for retention is line 85 (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:85), where the post-meeting emptiness is a new feeling Dan is trying to name and the negation-chain enacts his process of elimination. The closing passage (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:225) must use a different syntactic approach — render the emptiness through physical sensation, spatial detail, or a concrete image rather than through the same negation-chain. The weather instance (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:33) can be resolved by simply cutting 'not dramatic, just' and letting 'persistent' do the work: 'the persistent late-February cold of New York, the kind that found the gaps in your coat and stayed there.'

The line 123 instance (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:123), about the refrigerator shelf, can use a different vehicle — geological metaphor (sediment, erosion), gravitational metaphor, or simply render the habit directly without metaphor: 'though they'd never discussed this; it had organized itself through repetition.'

acceptance_test: Dan's interior monologue between lines 139 and 225 includes at least one moment of unguarded register — a profane thought, an uncensored impulse, or an internal sentence fragment that breaks his observational composure — consistent with his style-bible profanity profile. The moment should feel like a crack in control, not a performance of rawness.
evidence: Dan's interior monologue across the divorce scene (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:139-195) and the couch reflection (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:221-225) contains zero profanity, zero uncensored impulse, and no moment where his composure cracks internally. His style-bible profanity profile states: 'Moderate. Dan swears more when drinking or genuinely upset' with words like 'shit,' 'fuck,' 'goddamn.' In this scene, Dan is holding a beer (drinking) and learning his marriage is ending (genuinely upset), yet his interiority remains literary and composed: 'a relief so sudden that his grip on the beer bottle went slack' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:183), 'the dull fact that thirteen years and a child and a lease' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:183).
problem: Dan's emotional register in the divorce scene is too clean for his character. His stress tell — noticing irrelevant physical details — is correctly deployed (the condensation on the beer bottle, line 139). But the tell is only half the profile: under genuine upset, Dan should also have moments of raw, unguarded internal register. The chapter renders his emotional response entirely through controlled literary observation, giving him the interiority of a narrator rather than a forty-four-year-old man whose wife just told him she's filing for divorce while he's holding a beer. The absence of any profane or uncensored thought across this and all other scenes makes his private register indistinguishable from his public one — he's performing composure even inside his own head. This is timidity: the prose retreats from the full character at the moment the scene most demands it.
rewrite_direction: Insert at least one moment of unguarded internal register in the divorce scene (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:139-195). This does not mean a profanity-laden outburst — Dan's character is controlled. But a single internal 'shit' or 'fuck' that flickers through his mind before the composed observation resumes, or an unfinished internal sentence that his composure catches and redirects, would make his interiority feel inhabited rather than curated. The couch passage (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_01.md:221-225) is another candidate — Dan alone, processing, no audience for his composure, where a raw thought could surface briefly before the literary observation resumes. The key is contrast: one unguarded moment makes the surrounding composure feel like effort rather than nature.

acceptance_test: In the proxy-office section (lines 53-87), no more than 2 of 5 consecutive paragraphs use the trailing-clause architecture as their primary sentence shape. The effect is hypnotic and sometimes effective — it conveys the drift of proxy life — but when it becomes the default across office scenes, domestic scenes, and reflective passages alike, it stops being a tonal choice and becomes a structural habit. Cut or restructure instances where the comparison is generic or where a different syntactic form would work: direct metaphor ('her hand found the surface by memory' on line 189 works without 'the way you navigate a room'), temporal constructions, or simply ending the sentence. Highest-priority cuts: lines 193, 197, 255, 297, where the construction is doing little work beyond filling a syntactic slot.

acceptance_test: No more than 3 of the new characters introduced at the reception follow the template of physical-description-then-interpretive-gloss. At least 2 characters are introduced through dialogue, action, or ambient description without a narrator-delivered psychology reading. The reception scene feels like a room with people in it, not a sequence of curated character studies.
evidence: Every new character introduced at the reception follows the same template: physical description → Dan's interpretive psychology/symbolic reading. Anna Petrova (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:31): appearance → Dan's guilt. Art Pollard (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:73): bowtie/posture → 'the automatic warmth of someone who'd shaken ten thousand hands.' The bartender (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:89): locs/scar → 'the universal language of a bartender who understood.' The liaison (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:113): gray suit/laser part → 'the blankest smile.' The banana-couple women (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:115): paint under fingernails → Dan's respect. The South Asian woman at the table (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:71): bare foot on carpet → Dan's comfort. Six consecutive characters receive the identical treatment.
problem: The character-introduction formula (physical detail → narratorial psychological interpretation) is deployed identically for every new character at the reception. The reader comes to expect that each new face will arrive as a micro-essay: physical specifics, then Dan's analytical reading of what those specifics mean. This is Dan's narrative voice working correctly for one or two introductions, but six in sequence becomes mechanical. The reception should feel like a crowded, sensory-overloaded room, not a gallery of curated portraits.
rewrite_direction: Vary the introduction strategy. At least 3 of the 6 new-character introductions should use a different approach: (a) introduce through dialogue first, physical details arriving later mid-conversation (Art could be heard before he's seen); (b) introduce through action or gesture without the interpretive gloss (the barefoot woman is already effective without Dan's label of 'unreasonably comforting'); (c) introduce in aggregate as part of the room's texture rather than as individual portraits (the liaison could be a type rather than a described individual). Keep the full portrait treatment for at most 2-3 characters who earn extended observation. Target for restructuring: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:71, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:89, snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:113.

acceptance_test: The word 'inch' as symbolic/charged distance appears no more than 3 times in the chapter. The door-gap scene (around line 283) conveys its meaning without using the word 'inch.' The Mira section (around lines 289-291) renders phantom proximity through body sensation rather than naming and glossing 'the inch' as shared concept.
evidence: The word 'inch' as symbolic/charged distance appears 7 times in the chapter's final 45 lines: 'the inch between his mouth and her skin' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:257), 'the inch between them' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:259), 'Not even an inch' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:263), 'more charged than the inch in the car' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:275), 'An inch of space between the door and the frame, and the inch was deliberate' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:283, two uses), 'the inch — the inch in the car' and 'the same inch she was thinking about' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:289, 291).
problem: The 'inch' motif is powerful at first deployment (line 257, the distance between Dan's mouth and Mira's skin). By the seventh repetition, it has become a word the chapter is leaning on rather than an image working on the reader. The door-gap inch (line 283) explicitly glosses its own symbolism ('the inch was deliberate — closing the door would be a statement, leaving it open would be an invitation'). Then the Mira section names and re-glosses the same motif ('the same inch she was thinking about'). The image loses force through over-explanation and mechanical transference to new contexts.
rewrite_direction: Reduce 'inch' as charged symbolic distance to no more than 3 uses: the car almost-kiss (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:257), the lean-back (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:263), and one recurrence in the apartment sequence. The door-gap (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:283) should use a different unit of measurement or describe the gap without the word 'inch' — the image is already clear from 'Not closed. Almost closed.' The Mira section (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:289-291) should not name the motif; let Mira's body register the phantom proximity without explicitly referencing the 'inch' as a concept both characters are thinking about.

acceptance_test: If the Mira section is retained: a section break (---) precedes it, the POV stays in Mira's close-third throughout (no omniscient sentences narrating both characters), and the section contains at least one substantial perception or thought unavailable from Dan's POV. If the Mira section is cut: the chapter ends in Dan's close-third with a final image or sensation, not thematic summary.
evidence: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:289, the narrative shifts from Dan's close-third perspective ('He sat there and looked at the thin line of light beneath Mira's not-quite-closed door') to Mira's interiority ('In the bedroom, Mira lay on top of the covers in the black dress because taking it off would mean the evening was over') without a section break. The Mira section continues for 11 lines (289-299), then shifts further into omniscient summary: 'The apartment held two people who had spent an evening performing a marriage for machines' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:297).
problem: The style bible mandates: 'In shared-POV chapters, the rotation should be clear (section breaks or sustained passages in one POV before shifting) rather than head-hopping within a scene.' The shift to Mira's interiority at line 289 is a mid-scene POV break without a section divider. Additionally, lines 297-299 drift from Mira's close-third into an omniscient register that narrates both characters' experience from above, violating the close-third mandate. The Mira section also creates analytical redundancy — it re-renders the 'inch' motif and the ache of proximity that Dan's POV has already fully delivered.
rewrite_direction: Two options: (A) Insert a section break (---) before line 289, making the Mira section a clearly demarcated final movement. This requires cutting the omniscient summary at lines 297-299 and staying in Mira's close-third. (B) Cut the Mira POV section entirely and end the chapter in Dan's perspective — he watches the door close to its not-quite-closed position, sits on the couch with her perfume on his cuff, hears the refrigerator click, and does not move. The emotional resonance is already fully achieved in Dan's POV; Mira's section tells us what we already know. Option B is the stronger revision because it preserves the chapter's single-POV discipline and trusts the reader to infer Mira's parallel experience. If option A is chosen, the Mira section must not re-explain the 'inch' motif and must render genuinely new information (something Mira feels or thinks that Dan's POV could not have conveyed). Target: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:289-299.

acceptance_test: The cash amount Dan gives Anna at the end of the evening is mathematically consistent with the hourly rate established at line 31 multiplied by the number of hours she was present. The phrase 'more than the rate' is arithmetically true.
evidence: Line 31 establishes: 'Anna Petrova, sixteen, who charged twenty dollars an hour and who Dan always paid twenty-five' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:31). Line 269 states: 'She took the cash Dan held out — thirty dollars, more than the rate' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:269). Anna arrived at 6:30 PM (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:31). The Kesslers return from Midtown late in the evening (the reception empties, the car ride through Brooklyn is described); conservatively 10:30-11:00 PM. At $20/hr, the total owed is $80-90. At Dan's customary $25/hr, $112-125. Dan hands her $30 cash described as 'more than the rate.'
problem: The chapter establishes Anna's hourly rate and then pays her a lump sum that is roughly one-quarter to one-third of what she is owed for the evening's work. The phrase 'more than the rate' is internally contradicted by the chapter's own math. Any reader who noticed the $20/hr detail will do the arithmetic. This is either a babysitter-payment error or an inadvertent suggestion that Dan drastically underpays his childcare provider, which contradicts the characterization that he 'always paid twenty-five because he felt guilty.'
rewrite_direction: Fix the math at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:269. Two options: (A) Replace '$30' with a plausible total — e.g., 'a hundred and thirty dollars, more than the rate' (representing ~4.5 hrs at $25/hr, rounded up to reflect Dan's guilt-overpaying). (B) If cash-tip-on-top-of-digital-payment is the intended structure, establish this earlier (line 31 could mention that payments go through an app) so the cash at line 269 reads as a bonus. Option A is simpler. In either case, 'more than the rate' should survive only if the number supports it.

acceptance_test: The time reference for Dan's move to the couch is consistent with the story's timeline: late February (Chapter 1 events) to the chapter's internal March dating. Verify: the stated duration, when subtracted from the chapter's season markers, lands within the story's established start window.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:25 states Dan moved to the couch 'two months ago.' The continuity sheet's timeline places the mutual divorce agreement in 'Late February' (chapter_01) and Chapter 7's seasonal markers place it in late March: 'March was trying to become April' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:215). Late February to late March is approximately one month, not two.
problem: If Dan moved to the couch at or around the mutual divorce agreement (late February, Chapter 1), and the chapter takes place in late March, then 'two months ago' overstates the elapsed time by roughly 100%. This is a minor timeline inconsistency, but it is the kind of arithmetic-of-lived-time detail that attentive readers track across chapters. The continuity sheet's seasonal track ('Early spring — March into April' for chapters 5-9) and the in-chapter marker ('March was trying to become April') are consistent with each other but inconsistent with 'two months.'
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:25, either (A) change 'two months ago' to 'a month ago' or 'five weeks ago' or a vaguer formulation ('since he'd moved to the couch after the filing'), or (B) if Dan moved to the couch before the story's opening scene (i.e., before the mutual divorce agreement), establish this in the chapter's phrasing so the two-month timeline is defensible. Option A is simpler and consistent with the established timeline.

acceptance_test: The chapter's final 100 lines (approximately lines 200-300) contain at least 2 moments of dark comedy or absurdist awareness that arise from the premise without breaking the emotional arc. These moments should be legible as humor (a reader would recognize the comic note) and should coexist with the emotional intensity rather than deflecting it.
evidence: The chapter's comic register — sharp and alive in the reception sequence (the banana-optimization story at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:115-121, Art and Sheila's banter at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:73-87, the AI-designed party details at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:57-59) — goes dormant after approximately line 155. From the 'Tell me something' exchange through the car ride, the return home, and the final pages (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:157-299), the chapter operates in an unbroken register of earnest emotional intensity. No comic beats, no absurdist awareness, no dark humor about the premise. The novel's aesthetic risk policy states: 'The comedy must not protect them from the humiliation; the humiliation must not neutralize the comedy. Render both registers at full force, often in the same scene.'
problem: The chapter's second half sustains an emotional aria for approximately 140 lines without a single comic note. The premise of the scene — two estranged people who've spent an evening performing for robots now sitting in an AI-assigned sedan unable to stop wanting each other — has inherent absurdity that the prose does not access. The result is a tonal split: a comic first half and a solemn second half, rather than the dual-register integration the style bible demands. The emotional weight of the car scene and the return home would be intensified, not undermined, by moments of dark humor — Dan noticing the optimized tip, the AI-assigned car's navigation system, the absurdity of performing for machines and then suffering real consequences.
rewrite_direction: Introduce 2-3 comic beats into the car-ride and return-home sequences (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:211-299) without breaking the emotional throughline. These should be premise-derived dark humor, not deflection: the corporate card's optimized tip (already present at line 265 but played straight — let Dan or the narration find the absurdity in it), the driver's oblivious presence during a moment of marital crisis, the AI-assigned sedan as the least romantic setting for a rekindling, or the stairwell's choreography of avoidance (line 267 already has potential — 'both avoided it in synchrony' could register as funny rather than purely elegant). The comic register should be present as an undercurrent, not a competing tone — the way a person in real pain can still notice the ridiculous.

acceptance_test: The chapter's final 20 lines contain no sentences that explicitly interpret the symbolic meaning of a physical image already rendered in the scene (no 'this distance was more X than that distance because...' constructions). The chapter ends on a concrete image or sensation, not on thematic summary or omniscient narration of both characters' states. A reader can identify the chapter's emotional landing from the image alone, without the narrator's gloss.
evidence: The chapter's final 50 lines (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:250-299) accumulate analytical restatement. The hallway distance is glossed: 'the most consequential distance in the building, more charged than the inch in the car, because the car was outside and this was inside' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:275). The door gap is glossed: 'closing the door would be a statement, leaving it open would be an invitation, and the inch between was the exact width of everything they hadn't said' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:283). The Mira section glosses the evening's meaning: 'had discovered, inside the performance, something that was not a performance' (snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:297). Line 297-299 provide explicit thematic summary: 'They could not pretend the marriage was dead. They could not pretend they were only infrastructure.' Each of these passages tells the reader the meaning of an image that has already been rendered.
problem: The chapter's closing pages convert earned emotional images into thematic prose. The door left almost-closed is a powerful physical detail; then the narrator explains what it symbolizes. The hallway distance is evocative; then the narrator ranks it against the car distance. The effect is redundancy: the reader has already felt the weight of these images and is then told to feel it again, with labels. This is the prose equivalent of a filmmaker adding voiceover narration to a scene whose visuals already communicate. The chapter's emotional climax is dulled by over-explanation.
rewrite_direction: Cut or radically compress the analytical apparatus in snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_07.md:275-299. Specific targets: (1) The hallway-distance ranking sentence (line 275 from 'the most consequential' through 'the distance they'd wake up to') should be cut or reduced to the concrete detail only. (2) The door-gap gloss (line 283, 'closing the door would be a statement...') should be cut; 'Not closed. Almost closed.' already communicates everything. (3) Lines 297-299 should be cut entirely — they are thematic summary delivered from an omniscient vantage that violates close-third and tells the reader the chapter's meaning. End the chapter on an image or a sound, not a thesis. The stove light, the refrigerator click, and the perfume are all available as closing images that carry the chapter's weight without restating it.

acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than 4 instances of the 'the way [noun/pronoun] [verb]' comparative construction. No two surviving instances compare a domestic observation to a professional-past analogue. A reader encountering the fourth instance should not yet feel the pattern as predictable.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:3 ('absorbed into the apartment's operating rhythm the way the refrigerator's forty-minute click had been absorbed'), :5 ('the way she'd once mapped competitive landscapes'), :55 ('the way animals are aware of other animals at a watering hole'), :69 ('the way you could feel a sneeze building'), :71 ('the way other children carried stuffed animals'), :85 ('the way she'd once known organizational charts'), :113 ('the way Dan used to carry his phone'), :119 ('the way it had once mapped competitive positioning'), :285 ('the way she'd counted silences in meetings'), :289 ('the way she'd done for twelve years, the way a body learns another body's rhythm') — the comparative simile 'X the way Y' appears at least 11 times across 290 lines.
problem: The 'the way' comparative construction is the chapter's most pervasive prose formula. At 11 instances, it has crossed from stylistic choice into tic. Once or twice, it reads as Mira's analytical habit of cross-referencing — a character-true lens. At eleven times, every perception is funneled through the same syntactic mold, flattening the variety of Mira's intelligence and making the prose predictable. A reader can begin to feel the next 'the way' coming before it arrives, which is the definition of autopilot.
rewrite_direction: Reduce to no more than 4 instances across the full chapter. Retain the strongest: line 55 (the watering-hole comparison carries genuine observational force) and line 289 (the final-sentence breathing comparison earns its length as closure). Cut or restructure the remaining instances at lines 3, 5, 69, 71, 85, 113, 119, and 285. Replace with syntactically distinct constructions: direct metaphor, bare statement, subordinate clause, appositional phrase. No two surviving instances should share the same rhetorical function (i.e., no two 'domestic-thing compared to professional-thing' uses).

acceptance_test: The explicit domestic-to-professional comparison ('mapped/knew [home thing] the way she mapped/knew [work thing]' or equivalent) appears at most once in the chapter. Lines 85 and 119 convey Mira's analytical habit through the precision of observed detail rather than through stated comparison.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:5 ('She had mapped the building's water system the way she'd once mapped competitive landscapes, because mapping was what she did'), :85 ('She knew the whole apartment the way she'd once known organizational charts — every node, every connection, every load-bearing wall'), :119 ('mapping the apartment's soundscape the way it had once mapped competitive positioning')
problem: The specific formula 'Mira maps/knows [domestic thing] the way she once mapped/knew [professional thing]' appears three times in near-identical syntax. Each instance makes the same observation: Mira's strategic mind is now applied to her apartment. The first establishes this insight. The second and third repeat it without deepening or complicating it. By line 119, the reader has absorbed this facet of Mira's character and the third restatement reads as the prose reminding itself of something it already knows.
rewrite_direction: Keep one instance — line 5 is the strongest because it introduces the pattern early and includes the self-aware 'because mapping was what she did.' Rework lines 85 and 119 to show Mira's analytical frame through the quality and specificity of her perceptions rather than through an explicit comparison to her corporate past. At line 85, the apartment dimensions (11'2" x 9'4") and the classification document reference already demonstrate her analytical habit without needing the organizational-charts simile. At line 119, let the detail of Dan's nighttime sounds carry the analytical quality by themselves — the precision of '10:14,' '10:31,' '11:02' already does the work the mapping comparison tries to do.

acceptance_test: The word 'protocol' appears exactly 3 times: at its introduction (line 3 area), at the 1:47 a.m. subversion (line 125 area), and at the chapter's close (line 289 area). The three instances form a clear thesis-disruption-synthesis arc.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:3 ('protocols neither of them had written down'), :83 ('the not-asking had become its own protocol'), :125 ('no protocol covered 1:47 a.m.'), :145 ('another unwritten protocol'), :289 ('the only protocol no one had written down and everyone followed')
problem: The word 'protocol' appears five times as the chapter's thematic keyword. The word is doing real work — it bridges Mira's professional vocabulary with the domestic routines she can't stop systematizing — but five uses exhaust its resonance. The chapter's argument (that the apartment runs on unwritten rules that are both maddening and sustaining) is made more effectively by the rules themselves (the shower slot, the door-inches, the cabinet quadrants) than by repeatedly labeling them 'protocol.' By lines 145 and 289, the word has become its own unexamined protocol.
rewrite_direction: Reduce to 3 uses maximum. The strongest deployment is the arc from line 3 (introduction: 'protocols neither of them had written down') to line 125 (subversion: 'no protocol covered 1:47 a.m.') to line 289 (resolution: 'the only protocol no one had written down'). This creates a three-beat structure: rule, exception, deeper rule. Cut the word from lines 83 and 145 — at line 83, 'the not-asking had become its own ritual/silence/habit' or simply let the not-asking exist without categorization; at line 145, 'another unwritten rule' or drop the label entirely and let 'which was her cabinet' stand on its own.

acceptance_test: The explicit 'she knew [fact] because [reason]' or 'she hated that she knew [fact]' construction appears no more than 3 times in the first half of the chapter (lines 3-119). In at least 2 passages, Mira's hyperawareness is conveyed through the precision of the observed detail itself, not through narratorial declaration.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:3 ('Mira knew it was 6:15 because she had made it 6:15'), :7 ('She hated that she knew these sounds. She hated more that the sounds were comforting'), :9 ('she could have told you the exact width because she'd measured it once with her thumb'), :39 ('She knew the dimensions because the infrastructure classification document had included them'), :85 ('She knew this too. She knew the whole apartment'), :119 ('She was always listening. The analytical frame that was her professional reflex and her emotional armor did not switch off at night')
problem: The narration repeatedly declares Mira's hyperawareness rather than enacting it. Six times in the first 119 lines, the prose steps back from Mira's perception to announce that she is perceiving — 'she knew,' 'she hated that she knew,' 'she could have told you.' The effect is a narrator who keeps explaining the character's defining trait instead of trusting the reader to feel it through the quality of the observations themselves. The specificity of the details (6:15, four inches, 11'2" x 9'4") already demonstrates her hyperawareness. The declarations are redundant scaffolding.
rewrite_direction: In at least 3 of the 6 instances (target lines 39, 85, 119), remove or restructure the 'she knew' framing and let the observation do the work directly. For example, line 39: instead of 'She knew the dimensions because the infrastructure classification document had included them — 11'2" x 9'4",' try something like rendering the dimensions as part of Mira's perception of the space — the room's measurements surfacing in her mind unbidden, or noted with a specificity that tells the reader she knows them without declaring it. The first-person-address at line 9 ('She could have told you') is the most effective of the group because it has a rueful, confessional quality; keep it.

acceptance_test: Theo physically moves between Dan's zone and Mira's zone at least once during the chapter. The movement creates a moment where both parents are briefly connected through their child's trajectory — either looking at each other across his path, or responding to the same object/question he carries between them.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:55 ('Three bodies in an eleven-by-nine-foot room, each occupying a quadrant, each aware of the others' positions'), :85 ('Dan on the couch sat in the softer light of his laptop screen while Theo worked in the brighter pool and Mira, in the bedroom doorway, occupied the threshold between the two'). Chapter spine beat 2 specifies 'Theo as diplomatic bridge between his parents — his physical movement through the apartment connecting their separate zones.' In both the morning (lines 11-67) and evening (lines 85-115) sections, Theo remains stationary at the kitchen table while his parents orbit separate positions.
problem: The chapter spine requires Theo's physical movement to connect Dan's zone to Mira's, functioning as a diplomatic bridge. As written, Theo is a stationary center of gravity: he sits at the table in the morning and at the table in the evening. Both parents position around him, but he never moves between their territories. The chapter delivers Theo as a shared focal point but not as a bridge — a significant distinction. His conversational function (the math questions draw Mira into engagement, his drawing obsession becomes the 2 a.m. conversation's subject) partially compensates, but the physical-movement component of the beat is absent.
rewrite_direction: Add at least one moment where Theo physically moves between Dan's space and Mira's space. The morning section (lines 40-67) is the natural location: Theo could carry the signed permission slip to Dan (who's by the stove counter), then return to the table; or he could show a drawing to one parent and then cross the kitchen to get a reaction from the other. Alternatively, in the evening section, Theo could move between the couch (Dan's zone) and the bedroom doorway (Mira's threshold) — asking Dan a math question first, then crossing to ask Mira, his body tracing the path between their separate territories. The movement should feel incidental and child-natural, not choreographed.

acceptance_test: The near-passing moment (currently line 279) includes at least one sentence rendering a somatic response to Dan's proximity — a physical sensation located in Mira's body (pulse, breath, warmth, muscle tension) rather than only external sensory data (smell, distance). The passage maintains the chapter's quiet register; the desire is registered, not enacted.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:279 ('the space between them as she passed was six inches and he smelled like peanut butter and the soap from the shower he'd taken eight hours ago, a ghost of it still there in the cotton of his T-shirt, and she kept walking and she did not touch him'). The boundary context establishes that Chapter 7 ended with the almost-kiss and the recognition that the marriage was 'not dead' and that 'they were also, still, two people who wanted to be in the same room.' The style bible's intimacy_and_desire_policy requires rendering desire 'where in the body it lives, how it changes the air in a room.'
problem: The near-passing moment at line 279 is the chapter's key proximity scene, set after the almost-kiss of Chapter 7 has established that physical attraction is live. The prose renders proximity with Mira's characteristic precision — six inches, specific smells, the angled body — but reports entirely from the outside. We learn what she perceives (peanut butter, soap, cotton) but not what her body does with the perception. The not-touching is a deliberate, freighted act, but the reader doesn't feel its cost in Mira's body. This is timidity: the prose approaches desire and then retreats to sensory cataloging. The restraint of not-touching is more powerful if the reader knows what Mira's body wanted to do.
rewrite_direction: In the near-passing moment (line 279), add one or two sentences of somatic registration between the external detail and the 'she did not touch him' resolution. Where does the proximity land in Mira's body? Does her breathing catch? Does she feel warmth, a pull, a tightening? Does her hand do something involuntary? The register should stay quiet — this is not an eruption of desire but a physical fact she notices and manages. Additionally, the 'I wouldn't have' moment (line 265) could benefit from a brief physical dimension — something in her body that confirms what the sentence is reaching toward.

acceptance_test: All references to the Kessler marriage duration and shared-bed duration within the chapter are either numerically identical or accompanied by context that makes a different number self-evidently correct. A reader encountering the second number should not experience it as an error.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:119 ('a posture she'd spent twelve years of marriage lying beside'), :283 ('the bed they'd shared for eleven years'), :289 ('the way she'd done for twelve years')
problem: The chapter references 'twelve years' twice (lines 119 and 289) and 'eleven years' once (line 283) for the Kessler marriage or shared domestic life. While a plausible distinction exists (married twelve years, this specific bed for eleven), no context supports the difference. An attentive reader will parse line 283 as a continuity error — 'the bed they'd shared' reads as 'the marriage' in this context, making the eleven/twelve discrepancy conspicuous.
rewrite_direction: Unify to a single duration across all three references. If the marriage is twelve years, change line 283's 'eleven' to 'twelve.' If the bed genuinely predates the marriage by a year, add a brief contextual marker (e.g., 'the bed they'd bought the year after the wedding' or similar) that makes the distinction legible. Target: lines 119, 283, and 289.

acceptance_test: In the span from line 119 to line 289, no more than three sentences exceeding 80 words appear without an intervening sentence of 20 words or fewer. The final sentence of the chapter is the longest sentence in the closing two pages, preceded by at least one short declarative sentence within the final paragraph or the paragraph immediately before it.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_08.md:119 (sentence runs approximately 120 words), :247 (paragraph runs approximately 130 words as a single extended construction), :265 (sentence approximately 110 words), :283 (sentence approximately 100 words), :285 (sentence approximately 130 words), :289 (sentence approximately 100 words). In the chapter's final 170 lines, six sentences exceed 80 words each. The style bible's rhythm_target states: 'Avoid settling into any single rhythmic default — if three consecutive paragraphs use the same sentence structure, one of them is wrong.'
problem: The chapter's first half effectively varies rhythm — the morning dialogue scenes (Theo's cereal, the permission slip, Dan's departure) break up the longer reflective passages with short declarative sentences and clipped exchanges. The second half loses this variation. From the evening section onward (line 85+), the prose increasingly defaults to long, multi-clause sentences. The final pages (lines 279-289) are almost entirely composed of extended sentences, so that the closing sentence — which should be the chapter's most powerful rhythmic departure — arrives after five other sentences of similar length and loses its distinctive force.
rewrite_direction: Introduce rhythmic breaks in the back-half reflective passages. Primary targets: (1) Line 119's extended sound-catalogue sentence — break it into two or three sentences, using at least one short declarative to punctuate the list of Dan's nighttime sounds. acceptance_test: The word 'specific' (as an intensifier-adjective modifying a noun phrase) appears no more than twice in the revised chapter. At least one of the reworked passages conveys precision through concrete sensory detail rather than through the word 'specific' itself.

problem: The word 'specific' is used as an intensifier-adjective four times to signal that the narrator is rendering something precisely. The construction becomes self-undermining: by repeatedly announcing specificity, the prose advertises its own technique rather than simply being specific. The word does real work at line 69 (the bike messenger's calves) where the physical detail earns it. Elsewhere it functions as a verbal tic that says 'pay attention, this detail matters' when the detail should announce its own importance through selection and placement.
rewrite_direction: Retain 'specific' at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:69 where the physical detail earns the intensifier. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:5, cut 'specific' — 'a physical, unmistakably human problem' is stronger without it. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:95, replace with the sensory detail itself — show the hopefulness through Dan's body rather than labeling it. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:111, 'the numbness of having sat too long' is sufficient; 'specific' adds nothing the reader can't already feel.

acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than two 'which was [evaluative commentary]' constructions. The bathroom description at line 85 conveys humiliation through sensory detail or Dan's physical response rather than through an evaluative label.
evidence: Four instances of 'which was [evaluative commentary]' as parenthetical gloss: (1) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:7 'which was the worst part'; (2) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:17 'the building optimized for servers rather than bodies, which of course it was'; (3) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:85 'which was its own kind of humiliation'; (4) snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:107 'Which was everything.'
problem: The 'which was' parenthetical functions as a narrator's aside — a small editorial gloss appended to a description. Used once or twice, it's a natural feature of close-third narration where the character's evaluative voice overlaps with the narrator's. At four instances, it becomes a mannerism: the prose describes something, then appends a 'which was [judgment]' tag to make sure the reader caught the significance. The pattern is trust-eroding — it suggests the prose doesn't trust its own imagery to land without commentary.
rewrite_direction: Retain the strongest instance — snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:107 'Which was everything' works because of its sentence-fragment isolation and its position as the thematic pivot. For the other three: at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:7, fold the evaluation into the description rather than appending it ('not the volume of it but the practiced efficiency of a nine-year-old who knew when to withdraw — Theo's headphones going on before the voices even peaked'). At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:17, cut 'which of course it was' entirely; the reader already understands. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:85, rework so the humiliation is rendered rather than labeled.

acceptance_test: The revised passage at lines 147-165 does not describe direct sunlight entering through the kitchen window in the evening. Any directional light described is consistent with the east-facing kitchen window established in the continuity sheet. The temporal-visual clock (light narrowing, vanishing) is preserved through a spatially consistent light source.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:147 — 'The evening light came through the kitchen window and laid a long rectangle across the hallway floor, almost reaching his feet. He watched it shorten as the sun moved behind the brownstone across the street.' The continuity sheet states under environmental_constants: 'The apartment faces east; morning light comes through the kitchen window and hits the table where Theo eats breakfast.' An east-facing kitchen window receives morning light; it cannot receive evening light from the west.
problem: The chapter shows evening light entering through the kitchen window, but the continuity sheet establishes that the kitchen window faces east (receives morning light). Evening sun comes from the west and cannot enter an east-facing window. This is a spatial impossibility given the established apartment orientation. The detail is load-bearing — it provides the chapter's visual clock (the light narrowing and vanishing as evening progresses) across lines 147-165.
rewrite_direction: Two options for snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:147: (A) Move the light source to a west-facing window — the hallway or living room window could face west or south, allowing evening light. Dan could observe light coming through a different window from his position on the couch. (B) Replace the evening light with a different temporal marker — the quality of ambient light diminishing, streetlights activating, the shift from daylight to lamplight in the apartment. If using option A, the language at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:163 ('The rectangle of light narrowed to a bright line, then vanished') should track the revised light source consistently.

acceptance_test: The revised passage at lines 105-108 does not enumerate more than three items after 'Which was everything.' The revised pretzel cart passage at line 123 contains at least two sensory details specific to the cart/vendor and does not use the phrases 'optimization framework,' 'human-scaled,' or 'entirely legible.' The thematic contrast between the cart and the proxy world is communicated through what Dan perceives, not through analytical commentary.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:105-108 — 'He was weather. A warm front that had drifted through the building and would drift out again, producing no measurable effect on anything the system cared about. Which was everything. Which was the documents and the schedules and the optimization metrics and the resource alignments and the bilateral sensitivity parameters and whatever it was they were fighting about with Gemini-state — all of it, the entire enormous humming architecture of machine governance that Dan had spent the day trying to disrupt with his wrong signatures and his profanity and his extended lunch and his three-hour sit on a toilet he didn't need to use.' Also snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:123 — 'The pretzel cart was a thing that existed independently of any optimization framework. A man with a cart and a heating element and a purpose that was entirely legible: he made pretzels. People bought them. The transaction was human-scaled, complete, and visible from start to finish.'
problem: Both passages drift from close-third narration into essayistic register. At lines 105-108, the 'weather' metaphor is earned, but the subsequent enumeration ('the documents and the schedules and the optimization metrics and the resource alignments and the bilateral sensitivity parameters') reads as a thesis statement summarizing the chapter's argument rather than Dan's thought in the moment. At line 123, 'a thing that existed independently of any optimization framework' and 'The transaction was human-scaled, complete, and visible from start to finish' use analytical language ('optimization framework,' 'human-scaled,' 'transaction') that sounds like the narrator's gloss rather than Dan's interior voice. Dan's lexical signature per the style bible is concrete and bodily when honest — he would notice the pretzel smell, the man's hands, the cash exchange, not frame it as a thesis about legible purpose.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:105-108, trim the enumeration. The 'weather' metaphor and 'Which was everything' are strong enough to carry the meaning — the catalogue that follows over-explains. Cut or compress the list from 'Which was the documents...' through '...three-hour sit on a toilet he didn't need to use' to 2-3 concrete items that stay in Dan's physical experience of the day rather than abstracting it. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:123, anchor the pretzel cart observation in Dan's body and perception: what he sees (the man's hands, the cash drawer, the napkin dispenser), what he smells, what the simplicity feels like in his chest — rather than framing it as a proposition about optimization frameworks. Let the contrast emerge from the sensory detail, not from the narrator's analysis.

acceptance_test: The revised text from line 105 to the end of the chapter contains at least two moments where dark comedy coexists with Dan's emotional devastation — beats where the absurdity of his situation is legible alongside the pain, rather than the pain having fully displaced the humor. At least one of these beats occurs after line 131 (the homecoming). The final image of the chapter retains its quiet devastation.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:105-165 — From the 'weather' metaphor through the end of the chapter, the comic register that drives the first two-thirds of the chapter (the Robert Paulson signature, the profanity replacement, the 'personal maintenance time' notation) disappears entirely. The final sixty lines are straight devastation with no dark comedy. The chapter spine designates texture_mode as 'comic.' The style bible's register_commitment states: 'The comedy must not protect them from the humiliation; the humiliation must not neutralize the comedy. Render both registers at full force, often in the same scene.'
problem: The chapter's comic register peaks at line 103 ('Personal maintenance time') and then vanishes. The homecoming sequence (lines 131-165) is emotionally powerful but tonally uniform — quiet devastation without any of the dark-comic texture that distinguishes this novel's mode from conventional literary realism. The pretzel cart, the empty briefcase, the subway ads, the walk home — these are all opportunities for the kind of comedy-inside-despair that the chapter executes brilliantly in its first half (the system replacing profanity with compliance, the coffee timed to the elevator). The style bible warns against 'darkening the humiliation into dystopian grimness.' The final third tips into that territory.
rewrite_direction: The devastation of the ending is correct and should be preserved — Dan sitting in the dark, not telling Mira, is the right emotional destination. But the path there should maintain the novel's comic DNA. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:119-127, the empty briefcase and subway ads are rendered solemnly; find the bitter comedy in these details (the briefcase as prop, the streaming service ad for 'choosing' — these premises are inherently absurd and the prose should let that absurdity register even as Dan's misery deepens). At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_13.md:141-143, Dan's imagined speech to Mira is already halfway to dark comedy ('I'm furniture, Mira. I'm a chair that showed up in the wrong room'); let a beat of that dark humor land rather than immediately undercutting it with 'Not brave. Not angry. Not even sad.' The comic texture can coexist with the devastation — the chapter proves this in its first half.

acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than three instances of the 'not X, not Y' / 'not X, but Y' negative-litany construction. The final page (lines 193-209) uses the pattern at most once. At least two of the replaced instances achieve their emotional point through positive assertion, sensory detail, or action rather than negation.
evidence: Nine instances of the 'not X, not Y, but Z' / negative-litany formula: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:59 ('not optimized, not scheduled, not a prompt requiring confirmation'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:63 ('No one was monitoring... No one was going to send...'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:67 ('not data. That was not quantified, tracked, or correlated'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:107 ('not in her chest or her throat, nothing that theatrical'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:117 ('Not because she'd been deemed an unfit parent or because Theo was in danger or because any human being had looked at her life'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:167 ('Not closer. Not warmer.'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:195 ('not the distance of two people who had failed separately, but the distance of...'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:205 ('not because they'd chosen to, not because they wanted to, but because'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:209 ('not peacefully, not warmly, but with the weight of'). Three of these occur in the final fifteen lines, saturating the chapter's conclusion.
problem: The negative-litany construction -- defining what something is by listing what it is not, typically in a triple pattern -- is the chapter's dominant prose formula. At nine instances it has become a tic that flattens rhythmic variety. The construction is effective once or twice for philosophical precision; at nine it becomes the default means of generating profundity. The final stretch (lines 195-209) deploys it three times in quick succession, turning the chapter's emotional resolution into a rhythmic drone. The style bible mandates that 'if three consecutive paragraphs use the same sentence structure, one of them is wrong' -- here the same deep-structure recurs nine times across the chapter.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:195, :205, and :209, replace at least two of the three closing negative litanies with constructions that achieve the same meaning through assertion rather than negation. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:167, convert the fragment 'Not closer. Not warmer.' into a rendered observation or sensory detail. Across the full chapter, reduce total instances to no more than three, preserving the strongest deployments (line 117's systemic analysis is the best candidate to retain; line 59's description of non-optimized reading is the second). Strategy: where the construction currently explains what something isn't, find the concrete image or action for what it IS.

acceptance_test: No more than three consecutive paragraphs open with 'She [verb]' or 'Mira [verb].' Total 'She/Mira [verb]' paragraph openings in lines 3-75 do not exceed eight. At least three paragraphs in the morning-routine or coffee-shop sequences open with a non-subject construction (environment, object, sensory detail, or subordinate clause).
evidence: At least fourteen 'She [past-tense verb]' or 'Mira [past-tense verb]' paragraph openings in the first half: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:5 ('She read it'), :7 ('She showered'), :9 ('She dressed'), :11 ('She went to the kitchen'), :23 ('She did not confirm'), :25 ('She walked Theo to school'), :27 ('She turned left'), :31 ('She walked left'), :35 ('Mira ordered a coffee'), :37 ('Her phone buzzed... She reached in'), :39 ('She stood and looked'), :43 ('She sat back down'), :75 ('Mira turned her phone back on'), :103 ('Mira read it twice'), :109 ('She didn't. She put the phone').
problem: The 'She [verb]ed' anaphora creates rhythmic monotony across the chapter's first half. While there is an argument for intentional repetition -- a drumbeat of autonomous action during Mira's morning of freedom -- fourteen iterations bleeds from stylistic choice into default sentence construction. The resulting rhythm is metronomic: She did this. She did that. She did the next thing. The reader stops hearing the autonomy and starts hearing the formula.
rewrite_direction: In the morning-routine sequence at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:5-11, vary at least two of the four paragraph openings away from 'She [verb].' Options: lead with sensory detail ('The shower ran at 6:15'), lead with the object or environment ('Dark pants, a blouse she'd ironed'), embed the action in a subordinate clause. In the coffee-shop sequence at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:35-43, break at least two of the four 'She' openings. Preserve the anaphoric effect in a deliberate cluster of no more than three consecutive 'She' openings where the rhythm demonstrably serves the scene's momentum (the left-turn sequence at lines 27-31 is the best candidate for intentional anaphora).

acceptance_test: The revised chapter contains no more than two instances of 'the way [clause].' The three identified replacements at lines 133, 145, and 207 use distinct comparative constructions that do not repeat each other's grammatical shape.
evidence: Six instances of 'the way [clause]' as a comparative bridge: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:25 ('the way he always did'), :65 ('the way Theo's juice hadn't spilled'), :133 ('the way he processed things'), :145 ('the way you hear yourself recite a number'), :193 ('the way mothers had always known things'), :207 ('the way evenings did').
problem: 'The way [clause]' has become a connective formula -- a habitual bridge between observation and comparison. At six uses in one chapter it flattens the prose's comparative texture. Each instance reaches for the same grammatical structure to draw an analogy, creating a rhythmic fingerprint the reader begins to anticipate rather than experience.
rewrite_direction: Reduce to no more than two instances across the chapter. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:133, :145, and :207, replace with varied comparative constructions -- simile, metaphor, direct statement, or restructured syntax. Retain the two strongest deployments; lines 25 and 193 are the best candidates (one early, one late; one domestic, one thematic).

acceptance_test: 'Rectangle of light' appears no more than twice. 'Refrigerator' click/clicking appears no more than twice. 'Specific' as an emphatic adjective modifying an abstract noun appears no more than twice. Each surviving instance uses distinct phrasing from its pair.
evidence: 'Rectangle of light/sun' at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:63 ('The rectangle of sun on the floor moved from the counter toward Mira's table'), :111 ('The afternoon light making a long rectangle on the floor that crept imperceptibly east'), :119 ('the rectangle of light moved off the floor and climbed the far wall'). 'Refrigerator clicking/clicked' at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:11 ('The refrigerator clicking its forty-minute click'), :111 ('The refrigerator clicking'), :147 ('The refrigerator clicked'). 'Specific' as emphatic adjective modifying abstract nouns at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:63 ('the heat was specific and real and hers'), :111 ('the apartment's specific stillness'), :151 ('The specific, unperformable recognition'), :205 ('the specific, irreducible, stupid work').
problem: Three recurring motifs each cross the three-use threshold within one chapter. The rectangle of light is a time-tracking device that loses sensory freshness by its third deployment -- each use describes the same phenomenon (light moving across a surface) in nearly identical language. The refrigerator click is canonically meant to be beneath conscious notice (continuity sheet: 'absorbed into their sense of the apartment's rhythm without noticing it consciously'), but three explicit mentions make it a narrative tic rather than ambient texture. 'Specific' as an emphatic modifier -- meant to sharpen description -- dulls through repetition into a verbal placeholder for precision the prose hasn't actually achieved.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:119, cut or vary the rectangle-of-light image -- the coffee-shop instance at :63 and the apartment instance at :111 are stronger. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:147, cut or rephrase the refrigerator reference -- keep :11 for morning establishment and :111 for apartment emptiness. Reduce 'specific' as emphatic adjective to at most two of the four instances; :63 and :205 are the strongest candidates to retain (one sensory, one thematic).

acceptance_test: The passage at line 161 shares no more than three consecutive words with the passage at line 49. The emotional register of the second passage is audibly different from the first -- either compressed (shorter, more final) or reimagined (different metaphor or organizing principle). The dramatic shift from speculation to confirmation is legible in the new language.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:49 ('She didn't know the shape of his rebellion, but she knew the shape of its failure, because the shape was the same shape as every morning that followed') vs. snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:161 ('She didn't need the shape of his rebellion because she already knew the shape of its failure').
problem: The 'shape of rebellion / shape of failure' formulation appears twice with near-identical language sixty paragraphs apart. The first instance (line 49) is Mira speculating about Dan's rebellion before he confirms it. The second (line 161) is her recognition after Dan's admission. The dramatic context has shifted -- from speculation to confirmed mutual recognition -- but the language hasn't evolved to register the shift. The second instance restates the first rather than deepening it, reading as authorial autopilot rather than deliberate echo.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:161, rewrite to register the shift from speculation to confirmed recognition. Mira now has Dan's admission ('Nothing happened') to work with -- the second passage should land with the weight of confirmation, not repeat the speculative analysis. Options: make the second passage shorter and harder (she no longer needs to speculate, so the analysis collapses into a single blunt recognition), or shift the metaphor entirely (from 'shape' to a different organizing image that captures what it feels like when the guess you've been holding turns out to be true).

acceptance_test: The distance between Dan and Mira on the couch is elaborated in no more than one passage of analytical commentary (currently two at lines 167 and 195). The pasta exchange (lines 197-203) is followed by no more than two sentences of narrative commentary before the chapter transitions to the kitchen/evening scene. The chapter's final paragraph ends on a sensory or physical detail rather than a narrator-level summary of the day's events.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:167 ('Not closer. Not warmer. Something harder to name. The distance of two people sitting in the same debris, not touching, not needing to touch, just aware of each other's breathing and the shared knowledge that they had both, independently, pushed against a wall and the wall hadn't moved and the pushing had cost them differently but had taught them the same thing'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:195 ('not the distance of two people who had failed separately, but the distance of two people who knew they had failed at the same thing, and the knowing changed the air between them in a way that neither closeness nor coldness could account for'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:205 ('it was the language of two people still organizing a life together -- not because they'd chosen to, not because they wanted to, but because the apartment needed dinner and the child needed feeding and the evening needed to become night and no optimization engine had figured out how to automate the specific, irreducible, stupid work of being a person in a room with other people who also needed to eat'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:209 ('taking with it the coffee shop and the bad novel and the notifications and the four hours of breath and leaving behind the apartment, the couch, the pasta water, the child drawing behind his closed door, the two people who lived here because they had not yet found a way to stop').
problem: The chapter's second half over-narrates its own emotional significance. Lines 167 and 195 both elaborate the same 'distance' metaphor into analytical commentary that tells the reader what the couch scene means rather than letting the rendered scene -- the dialogue, the silences, the hand on the cushion -- carry its own weight. Line 205 states the chapter's thematic point as a thesis sentence. Line 209 catalogues the chapter's events into a retrospective summary. Mira's analytical voice justifies some interpretation, but the chapter defaults to it so heavily that the scenes serve as illustrations of their own commentary. The style bible says the narrator 'should not editorialize on the language'; this is editorializing on emotion -- the prose explaining feelings the scene has already conveyed through action and dialogue.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:167, cut or compress the distance elaboration to two sentences maximum -- the rendered detail ('the same three cushions,' the awareness of breathing) is stronger than the analysis. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:195, cut entirely or reduce to a single sentence -- it restates the insight from :167 in the same metaphor. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:205, trust the pasta exchange to carry its own weight; cut after 'a life together' or reduce the explanatory tail to one clause. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:209, end the chapter closer to the sensory detail (pot lid, humming, car passing) and cut the narrator's retrospective catalogue of the chapter's events.

acceptance_test: The couch scene (lines 129-207) contains at least one moment where Mira registers Dan's physical presence involuntarily -- through a sensory detail located in her body (warmth, smell, spatial awareness of his weight on the cushion). This moment is no longer than two sentences and is not immediately followed by a negation or dismissal. The scene's overall emotional architecture (recognition through shared failure, maintained distance) is preserved.
evidence: snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:167 ('Not closer. Not warmer.'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:171 ('Not reaching for Dan. Just pressing her palm against the fabric, grounding herself on something solid'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:175 ('He didn't take it. But he looked at it'), snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:195 ('She didn't lean back. The distance stayed what it was.').
problem: The style bible's intimacy_and_desire_policy mandates that 'Dan and Mira's residual physical attraction is a narrative force, not background radiation' and requires rendering desire 'with the same specificity as any other emotional state -- where in the body it lives, how it changes the air in a room.' The couch scene achieves deep emotional intimacy through shared failure and mutual recognition, yet it systematically negates any flicker of physical awareness. Every physical detail is accompanied by a negation: not reaching, not taking, not leaning, not closer. In a scene of genuine vulnerability between two people with fifteen years of physical history -- a scene whose emotional temperature is higher than any they've shared in weeks -- the complete absence of even involuntary physical awareness reads as the prose retreating from the body. The chapter doesn't need a kiss or a romantic beat; it needs one moment where proximity registers in Mira's body before the scene continues.
rewrite_direction: At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:171 or :175, add one beat of involuntary physical awareness -- Mira registering Dan's proximity through a sensory detail located in her body (his warmth across the cushion distance, the specific post-workday smell of him, the memory-flash of what his hand feels like) before the scene continues. The awareness should be brief (one to two sentences), specific, and not acted upon. It should complicate the 'distance' the scene is otherwise about, not resolve it. Do not add an almost-kiss or a romantic gesture -- add a body registering another body's presence the way fifteen years of marriage makes involuntary.

acceptance_test: The notification sequence (lines 79-117) contains at least two moments where the prose's register simultaneously conveys the absurdity and the cruelty of the institutional response -- where the reader can recognize the dark comedy of the disproportion even as the devastation lands. Mira's analytical voice engages with the comedy of the cause-consequence gap (reading a novel vs. losing parental portal access) at least once as comic recognition, not only as strategic or emotional analysis.
evidence: Chapter-wide. The bureaucratic notifications at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:85, :93, :101 use institutional language that is inherently absurdist ('ROUTINE SECURITY MEASURE,' 'PRECAUTIONARY,' 'DOES NOT CONSTITUTE A PUNITIVE DETERMINATION') but the prose renders them exclusively for devastation. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:95 ('The language was immaculate. It managed to freeze her access to her own bank account while assuring her, in syntactically perfect sentences, that nothing bad was happening'), the narrator identifies the absurdity but plays it entirely straight. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:115 ('Three days without access to her money or her son's school records because she'd sat in a coffee shop and read a novel for four hours'), the sentence contains the absurdist juxtaposition but renders it only as cruelty.
problem: The novel's creative_risk_policy mandates that 'The comedy must not protect them from the humiliation; the humiliation must not neutralize the comedy. Render both registers at full force.' The register_commitment declares the comic register 'IS the message.' While this chapter's texture_mode is 'tense then cold,' the policy explicitly warns against 'darkening the humiliation into dystopian grimness.' The notification sequence -- a system freezing a mother's access to her child's school portal through language that insists it is not punishing her -- is prime dark-comedy material played with unbroken solemnity. The chapter has scattered moments of dry wit (line 59 on the novel's metaphors, line 163 on competitive suffering, line 205 on the 'stupid work'), but these are muted and outnumbered by solemn analytical passages. A strategist of Mira's intelligence would find the comic disproportion between cause and consequence -- even as it devastated her.
rewrite_direction: In the notification sequence at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:79-107, let Mira's analytical voice find the comedy in the bureaucratic language without reducing the devastation. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:95, allow the observation about 'syntactically perfect sentences' to register as both funny and cruel simultaneously -- Mira can appreciate the machine's rhetorical precision even as it wrecks her. At snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:115, let Mira's internal voice register the comedy of the disproportion (book in a coffee shop vs. loss of parental access) as dark-comic recognition, not only as strategic analysis. The target is the wince-then-laugh (or laugh-then-wince) the creative_risk_policy calls for -- comedy that makes the cruelty worse, not comedy that softens it.

acceptance_test: At least two of Dan's lines in the couch scene carry a distinctive Dan marker: either a hedging qualifier that he starts and cuts short, a reflexive corporate word he hears himself using, or a concrete physical observation that reveals his specific perceptual habit. Line 159 already works as stress-tell repetition. At least two other lines should sound recognizably like Dan Kessler and no one else in the novel.
evidence: Dan's dialogue at snapshots/cycle_01/chapters/chapter_14.md:135, :139, :143, :149, :159, :165, :177, :181, :187, :199, :203. Of eleven spoken lines, ten are confirmatory echoes or simple questions: 'They froze your accounts,' 'And the school portal --,' 'Because you didn't go to work,' 'Was it worth it?' 'Yeah. Yours was worse,' 'Four hours,' 'Will you? Go back?' 'OK,' 'I'll do it,' 'I'll make pasta.' Only line 159 ('Nothing. Nothing happened. That's what happened.') carries a distinctive voice marker (the stress-tell repetition specified in the style bible).
problem: The style bible specifies Dan's syntax signature as 'medium-length sentences with qualifications and false starts' featuring hedging words ('well,' 'sort of,' 'I mean,' 'the thing is'). Under genuine upset he 'gets shorter and more direct, which should feel like a departure from his baseline.' The departure IS present -- Dan is short and direct -- but without any baseline contrast within the scene, the reader cannot feel it as departure. His clipped quality reads as generic listening rather than Dan-under-pressure. His lexical signature (corporate vocabulary he can't fully shed, physical/grounded language when honest) is entirely absent from his eleven lines. His dialogue could belong to any sympathetic listener in any novel.
